Why don't we omit it at all? Slice would use the Integer (floor) number of it, wouldn't this be fine too?

author: BHesseldieck

body:
Thanks for bringing this up - in fact, it does get the integer part only. I changed to `Math.ceil` to make it clear that we're always rounding up not to generate confusion.

The reason not to omit is that more characters is always better than less. Esp for shorter strings, it makes a lot of difference, so I think using ceil works best.
author: krynble

body:
If you want to have more than you should use `Math.floor` or even omit the rounding, since slice will round down and take the integer value of it example:

```js
var password = '1234567891011'
password.slice(Math.ceil(password.length / 2)) --> '891011'
password.slice(Math.floor(password.length / 2)) --> '7891011'
password.slice(password.length / 2) --> '7891011'
```

@Joffcom sorry for the delay ! 

It's not easy to share an example workflow because I obviously have specific node which read encoded protobuf and parse it it JSON, and I can't share it here. 

But my workflow is like that : 
```
{ "nodes": [ { "parameters": { "topic": "identity-events", "groupId": "n8n-client-id", "options": {} }, "name": "Kafka Trigger", "type": "n8n-nodes-base.kafkaTrigger", "typeVersion": 1, "position": [ 460, 300 ], "credentials": { "kafka": { "id": "1", "name": "Kafka account" } } }, { "parameters": { "message": "message", "targetField": "identityEvent" }, "name": "Identity Event Decoder", "type": "@swan-io/n8n-nodes-swan.identityEventDecoder", "typeVersion": 1, "position": [ 680, 300 ] }, { "parameters": {}, "name": "NoOp", "type": "n8n-nodes-base.noOp", "typeVersion": 1, "position": [ 900, 300 ] } ], "connections": { "Kafka Trigger": { "main": [ [ { "node": "Identity Event Decoder", "type": "main", "index": 0 } ] ] }, "Identity Event Decoder": { "main": [ [ { "node": "NoOp", "type": "main", "index": 0 } ] ] } } }

```

The node `Identity Event Decoder` will transform a protobuf buffer (I use https://github.com/stephenh/ts-proto for encoding/decoding) in a JS object. 

And with the default behaviour, I think the toString method can alter buffer sometimes, and then the decoding no longer works

body:
In addition to the dir case, there are potentially more levels of nesting: dir > component > symbol > toast. Rephrasing a display string, renaming a component or dir, refactoring a component, they all require updating the translation file.

I don't disagree that this coupling is not ideal, but if we flatten the object, the translations file becomes less organized, and we lose the ability to translate the same keys in different ways when in different contexts. Enforcing nesting to avoid key clashes was the motivation for reworking how nested node params are translated in the latest i18n refactorings.

body:
Renamed as suggested, but please consider:
- File searchability. This is a monorepo where we already have four different `credentials.ts` files that match on file search - even searching for `credentials name` (file plus part of new dirname) actually brings up `CredentialsSelectModal.vue` first. On the other hand, using a dotted suffix like `.endpoints` brings up the file already on `credentials.e`, followed also by the relevant tests `credentials.endpoints.test.ts`, with all other similar but irrelevant `credentials` files getting filtered out. We could use `.api` instead of `.endpoints` to keep it concise, ideally mirrored in the tests file.
- Consistency. I think this bundle of endpoints does not constitute a namespace (e.g. thinking of wildcard `*` imports, TS types grouped in namespaces, etc.) because  in `Server.ts` we are not referencing them as `<namespace>.<entity>`. These endpoints are not namespaced, just split off into a separate file.

body:
1. Curious why a chokidar setup instead of [esbuild watch mode](https://esbuild.github.io/api/#watch)? I expect this rebuilds on changes to any file, not just TS files, i.e. more than what we need?
2. Should we rebuild on `unlink`?
3. If `ignoreInitial` is `false`, it glitches out by re-detecting constantly. If `ignoreInitial` is `true`, then building before watching becomes required, i.e. if we watch without building first, then no `/dist` is created until the first change. This differs from the behavior of `tsc --watch`, which knows to build if there is no `/dist`.

body:
Thank you for the changes. Some final comments:

1. It is unusual that we only need to build `/nodes-base` in isolation. `/nodes-base` is almost always built together with all other packages  via `dev` in the root `package.json`, i.e. `lerna exec npm run dev --parallel`, which will run `npm run build` in `/nodes-base`. This means that `npm run build:fast` is unlikely to be used in day-to-day work. Should we add a root command for fast builds?

2. `skipLibCheck` will also skip checking of declaration files in the project itself, which is relevant because many nodes contain custom `.d.ts` files. The best of both worlds would be enabling this flag for the speed boost, but also converting all `.d.ts` files to `.ts` to ensure they're type-checked. Context: [1](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/wiki/Performance#skipping-dts-checking) [2](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#skipLibCheck) [3](https://www.testim.io/blog/typescript-skiplibcheck/) [4](https://twitter.com/orta/status/1260583041740812288) Maybe we should consider delaying this flag until we can take the time to convert them.

3. `build.mjs` is throwing off ESLint:

```
Parsing error: "parserOptions.project" has been set for @typescript-eslint/parser.
The file does not match your project config: packages/nodes-base/scripts/build.mjs.
The extension for the file (.mjs) is non-standard. You should add "parserOptions.extraFileExtensions" to your config.
```

From a quick check, mjs needs to be [added to `extraFileExtensions`](https://github.com/typescript-eslint/typescript-eslint/issues/3383) or converted to `mts` which is supported by default, or else we could disregard mjs in `.eslintignore`.

4. `build:typecheck` is now inlining multiple flags to avoid using `--project`. These flags are a mix of settings already present in `tsconfig.json` e.g. `module`  and of settings absent from `tsconfig.json`, e.g. `downlevelIteration` and `allowSyntheticDefaultImports`. I am reluctant to introduce duplication and add new compiler flags that were not needed before and that required changes to the source as in Magento and Action Network, for a 2s gain. Commenting mostly to bring up the concern, but up to you.

5. Why switch `module` and `target` to ESNext? From the TS docs: `The special ESNext value refers to the highest version your version of TypeScript supports. This setting should be used with caution, since it doesn’t mean the same thing between different TypeScript versions and can make upgrades less predictable.`

6. `fast-glob` is listed twice in `nodes-base/package.json`, as regular dependency and as dev dependency.

7. `"test/**/*"` is being included and `"**/*.spec.ts"` is being excluded but all tests in `/nodes-base` are JS.

body:
**General question**: If `^build` makes any package's `build` depend on its dependencies' `build` having completed first, [as explained here](https://turborepo.org/docs/core-concepts/pipelines#topological-dependency), then why do we need to specify our chain of dependencies by hand with the keys below? And why does the specified list of dependencies in `turbo.json` not reflect the dependency lists in `package.json` in our various packages?
- `design-system` does not depend on any package, but `turbo.json` lists `core` as a dependency.
- `editor-ui` depends on `design-system` and `workflow`, but `turbo.json` lists `core` as a dependency.
- `cli` depends on `workflow`, `core` and `editor-ui`, but `turbo.json` again lists `core`.

(Never used Turborepo before, there is probably a reason, mostly curious.)

**Smaller comments**:
1. Turborepo always needs `outputs` to be specified and is unable to find this in `tsconfig.json`?
2. `format`, `lint` and `lintfix` are not included in `turbo.json`. `test` is included, but it is still set to run with lerna in the root `package.json`.
3. We do not use `tsx`.
4. `full` is the [default output mode](https://turborepo.org/docs/reference/configuration#outputmode) and so does not need to be specified.
5. Re: `inputs`, not all code relevant to tests is in `/src` and `/test`, e.g.`/cli/commands`, `/cli/config`, `/nodes-base/nodes`, etc.
6. `"n8n-editor-ui#build"` When using `vue-cli-service build`, I notice it reports `Using 1 worker with 2048MB memory limit`. Since `editor-ui` is one of the slowest building packages, can we experiment with [adding workers](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/62598443/how-to-increase-performance-when-building-vue-webapp) and [raising the memory limit](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/55258355/vue-clis-type-checking-service-ignores-memory-limits)?

body:
The schemas endpoint does only return custom objects, but the API documentation states that the objects API can also be used with contacts and other HubSpot objects. I did not find a Hubspot api endpoint, that gave me all objects, so I just hardcoded contact for now. You can generate the full list of possible objects with something like `seq 50 | xargs -n1 -i{} curl 'https://api.hubapi.com/crm/v3/schemas/0-{}?hapikey=INSERT_API_KEY_HERE' 2>/dev/null | jq '{name: .labels.singular, value: .objectTypeId }'`.

It would probably be best to add the full list here, as that would allow generic access to all HubSpot objects, but it would also clutter the list and make the custom objects harder to find.
author: zebreus

body:
When an element in a batch operation fails, the other elements still get processed. It seems to be common practice in most n8n nodes, to stop execution on error and not process any item after the erroring one.
The batch operations do process the upcoming items (because requests are batched), so the behaviour is not quite what the user would expect, when `continueOnFail` is disabled.
author: zebreus

body:
I want to return some additional details such as the id of the failed object, because just the error message returned by HubSpot is often generic. When `continueOnFail` is disabled, this is fine, because the whole response is displayed inside the spoiler. If `continueOnFail` is enabled only the message is shown and there is no way to retrieve further details about the error for logging.

For example any error during an update returns the message `Bad request - please check your parameters`. The response from Hubspot contains a lot more information about the error, to get and display that in a sane way it needs to be parsed.

The parsing can probably simplified a bit, but it should work for now.
author: zebreus

body:
The first check is just there, because the batch operations don't behave in the expected way, if continue on fail is not set. It could basically be removed and the code would still work.

This check checks it again because here it is actually wrong to parse additional information and return it if we don't `continueOnFail`. It cannot be removed.
author: zebreus

body: No. If `idProperty` is set we are using a custom id property, which is not supported for all objects api operations. `DELETE` does not support it, but `GET` does, so a `get` request needs to be done first to resolve the custom id property id, to the HubSpot object id.

Hey @michael-radency,
thanks for your review :rocket:. I have addressed all your comments above.

I left the threads for the continueOnFail check and the error parsing unresolved and stated the reasons for the decisions in the comments.

I have noticed that you added a operation that uses the schemas api. I think the schema operations should be summarized under a new ressource, because they do not operate with objects, but with schemas. Also the list of valid object types is different for the schemas and the objects api, as schemas can operate on nearly all types, while objects cant. I already have written code for resources for properties, property groups, and associations and will open a PR for that as soon as this PR is merged (Because the other commits depend on this). I think it would be best to include schemas there and not here.

The update operation lets the user specify they updated values as a fixedCollection of key/value pairs. One problem I noticed with that the node gets really sluggish when there are a lot of entries, I suspect it has something to do with the loadOptions method and the amount of options. Do you have any idea for an elegant solution to this?

Oh, nice. I didn't catch that. In that case, I don't see a reason that you wouldn't want to `nack` it, so you probably don't need the option. It should just always send that nack if it can't parse a message.

Does that deferred promise on the `emit` normally get the response of the last node in the workflow, and reject it if the workflow fails anywhere? I can take a look at implementing something like that for the emit later, but it's probably not needed to merge this in.
author: Ken-Michalak

body:
I think this is going to close every RabbitMQ connection when one workflow (actually one node) is stopped. The connections are 1 per set of credentials and running process, and should only be closed when the entire n8n process shuts down. (You could attempt to track the number of consumers per connection and shut it down, but I thought that would be difficult to track accurately and could lead to bugs.)

https://www.cloudamqp.com/blog/part1-rabbitmq-best-practice.html#connections-and-channels

When you have multiple workflows with rabbitmq nodes and multiple workers, you don't want to open and close a connection for every node, which is why there's a global map of connections.

> 
> If we could nack the message if the workflow failed somewhere down the line, that'd probably be ideal, but I couldn't see a way to do that.

and based on the workflow's result (or some data returned by the last node in the workflow), decide to ack or nack the message, right?

body:
`displayParameter` works as expected, i.e. for HTTPRN v1, it returns `false` for the cred of the same name that should be shown for v2. The issue is `find`. Because the creds in the two versions have the same names, and because the v2 creds are located first, `find` finds always the v2 cred for HTTPRN v1, and so `displayParameter` correctly reports that the v2 cred should not be shown for HTTPRN v1, without the cred with the same name in the other version being checked. Therefore, the fix filters creds by version before `displayParameter` is reached.

Can you explain why this is wrong?

body:
To make this component even more reusable for the future, we should have a slot that we can use to place content at the top-right.

~~~
<slot name="actions" />
~~~

Then, we'd be able to use this callout component and put anything (not just a link) at the top right in the future.

~~~html
<n8n-callout>
  <template #actions>
                 <n8n-link
			:to="/something"
			size="small"
			theme="secondary"
			:bold="true"
			:underline="true"
		>
			Some text
		</n8n-link>
  </template>
</n8n-callout>
~~~
author: alexgrozav

body: We should use `v-bind="$attrs"` only on a root-level element, because this will assign all external attributes to the element and can lead to duplicate ids, repeating class names and unwanted attributes.

- can have text emphasis (bold, italic)

body:
1. For learning, why local storage instead of a Vuex store value coming from the `settings` column in the `user` table? Do we want to make it easy to keep this hint showing every time local storage is cleared?
2. Ideally, `dataItemsCount` integer should be checked before querying local storage.
3. `LOCAL_STORAGE_PIN_DATA_INITIAL_DISCOVERY_WORKFLOW_FLAG` Should we specify the medium `LOCAL_STORAGE` in the constant name? A generic name would prevent us from having to rename when refactoring later.
4. `LOCAL_STORAGE_PIN_DATA_INITIAL_DISCOVERY_WORKFLOW_FLAG` Does `INITIAL` add anything here? Discovery happens only once, at least until local storage cleared.
5. `LOCAL_STORAGE_PIN_DATA_INITIAL_DISCOVERY_WORKFLOW_FLAG` I find `CANVAS` instead of `WORKFLOW` more accurate + descriptive.
6. `hasSeenPinDataTooltip`, `showPinDataDiscoveryTooltip`, etc. Should we mention the medium `Tooltip`? A generic name like `Hint` prevents us from having to rename based on later refactorings, as had to be done for `.edit-mode-footer-buttons` → `.edit-mode-actions`
7. `checkPinDataDiscoveryFlow` The main purpose of this method is updating local state to show the hint, the checks are only there to make sure this happens in specific conditions. `showPinDataHint`?
8. Matter of preference, L365-7 could be a single line.
9. `5000` In my experience, 5 seconds was not nearly enough, because you first have to realize that there is a new tooltip to read, which is easy to miss in the NDV and especially when you're alt-tabbing back into the NDV from where you made the request to manually trigger the trigger node. Also the wording is not simple enough for a flashing tooltip: `You can pin this data to avoid having to generate an event every time.` Maybe 10 seconds with `You can pin the output of this node [Learn more]` with tooltip kept open on hover.

body:
From a DX point of view, I would expect our `n8n-checkbox` component to work with `v-model` as well, just like the `el-checkbox`.

Here we could bind the `value` prop of our component, and re-emit the `input` event. Also, don't forget add a `value` prop. 

~~~html
:value="value"
@input="$emit('input', $event)"
~~~

Having the `value` prop + an `input` event will allow us to use `v-model` on `n8n-checkbox`. Other than that, great job on this! :)
